metadata.title: Economic Systems
metadata.subtitle: How Capitalism, Socialism, and Mixed Economies Shape Your Financial Opportunities

content.title: Economic Systems
content.subtitle: How Capitalism, Socialism, and Mixed Economies Shape Your Financial Opportunities

content.label: The Challenge
content.question:
The Martinez family's story spans three generations and two economic systems. In 1960, Carlos fled Cuba after Castro's revolution nationalized his father's grocery business.

Arriving in Miami with $300, Carlos worked two jobs, saved aggressively, and within 5 years opened a bodega. Over 30 years, he built a business worth $600,000. His daughter Elena expanded to 3 locations with 35 employees.

Meanwhile, relatives who stayed in Cuba earn $30-50/month with no path to wealth accumulation.

Same family, same work ethic—vastly different results. How does your economic system determine your opportunities?

slides.notes:
**Discussion Prompts:**
- Why did Carlos succeed in America but his relatives couldn't succeed in Cuba with the same work ethic?
- What specific features of the U.S. system enabled Carlos to build a $600,000 business?
- How do economic systems create vastly different opportunities?

objectives.description: Define and compare capitalism, socialism, and mixed economies and their core characteristics.
Explain the difference between command (centrally planned) and market economic systems.
Analyze how property rights function and enable wealth building in different systems.
Evaluate how economic systems affect personal financial opportunities and career paths.

terms.definition: Economic system where private individuals and businesses own resources; prices determined by supply and demand; profit motive drives activity.
terms.example: In a capitalist system, Carlos could start a bodega, keep his profits, and build wealth over decades.

terms.definition: Economic system where government owns most resources and makes centralized production and distribution decisions; emphasis on equality over efficiency.
terms.example: In Cuba's socialist system, the state owns businesses and assigns jobs with fixed wages.

terms.definition: System combining capitalism and socialism; most resources privately owned but government provides public goods, regulates markets, and redistributes some income.
terms.example: The US has private businesses alongside public schools, Social Security, and FDA regulation.

terms.definition: Legal ownership and control over resources; foundation of market economies enabling exchange, investment, and wealth accumulation.
terms.example: Property rights let you use your home as collateral for a business loan—impossible without them.

leftPanel.title: Who Decides What Gets Made?
paragraphs: Every society must answer fundamental economic questions:
Different economic systems answer these questions in dramatically different ways—and those answers profoundly affect your career prospects, business opportunities, and wealth-building potential.

highlightBox.text: The U.S. operates as a mixed economy, blending market mechanisms with government intervention.

leftColumn.title: Capitalism (Market System)
items: Prices set by supply and demand

layoutData.title: Market Allocation vs. Command Allocation
paragraphs: The fundamental economic problem: unlimited human wants facing limited resources. Every society must decide what to produce, how, and who gets it.
bulletPoints: Market Allocation: Decentralized decisions by millions of people. Prices signal what's scarce (high price) or abundant (low price). No central planner needed—the "invisible hand" coordinates.
Command Allocation: Central planners decide production and distribution. Without price signals, planners lack critical information. Often results in wrong sizes, colors, chronic shortages.
Example: High smartphone demand → high prices → entrepreneurs invest → more phones → prices fall. That's market allocation working automatically.
Contrast: Soviet planners decided to make millions of left shoes but forgot right shoes. Without prices to signal demand, they couldn't coordinate effectively.

keyPoint.text: 📊 Prices convey more information than any planner can process—they coordinate an economy automatically.

leftPanel.title: Why Property Rights Matter
paragraphs: Without secure property rights, wealth-building is impossible.
Without property rights: Why save if it can be seized? Why invest if you can't keep profits? Why lend if you can't foreclose?

stats.label: Carlos started his business with this

leftPanel.title: The Data Is Clear
paragraphs: Countries with more economic freedom consistently achieve:
The lesson: Economic system matters more than natural resources. Singapore (no resources) became rich; Venezuela (massive oil) became poor.

highlightBox.text: South Korea vs. North Korea: Same people, same culture, same resources—19x income difference.

stats.label: South vs. North Korea income gap

slides.notes:
## Common Misconceptions

| Misconception | Clarification |
|---------------|---------------|
| "The USA is pure capitalism." | The USA is a mixed economy with significant government roles: public education, Social Security, Medicare, environmental regulations, antitrust laws, etc. About 25% of GDP involves government spending. |
| "Socialism means everyone is equal and prosperous." | While socialism aims for equality, historical command economies (USSR, Cuba, North Korea) produced widespread poverty and shortages. Equality often meant everyone equally poor (except political elites). |
| "Capitalism causes all poverty and inequality." | While market economies can produce inequality, they've also produced unprecedented poverty reduction globally. Most poverty occurs in countries with weak market institutions and property rights, not strong capitalist systems. |

content.headerTitle: Your Opportunities in a Mixed Economy

layoutData.title: What the U.S. System Means for You
paragraphs: In the U.S. mixed economy, your financial success depends on your decisions—within the framework the system provides:
bulletPoints: Career Freedom: Choose your profession, negotiate your salary, change jobs freely. No government assignment.
Business Ownership: Start a company for $2,000-$10,000 in filing fees. Keep your profits after taxes.
Investment Options: Stocks, bonds, real estate, retirement accounts—multiple paths to wealth building.
Credit Access: Property rights enable mortgages and business loans. Use assets as collateral to grow.
Safety Net: Unemployment insurance, Social Security, Medicare provide backstop—but wealth building is your responsibility.

keyPoint.text: 💼 The system provides opportunity—but YOU must take advantage of it through education, saving, and smart decisions.

scenario.name: Maya's Career Choices
paragraphs: Maya graduates with a computer science degree. She has job offers from three companies, could freelance, or start her own tech company.
In a command economy, she would be assigned to work where the government needs programmers.

scenario.name: James's Business Journey
paragraphs: James wants to open a coffee shop. He has $25,000 saved and needs $75,000 more.
In the US, he can get an SBA loan using his property as collateral. In a weak property rights system, no lender would risk it.

outcomes.label: Weak Property Rights
outcomes.detail: Lenders won't risk without foreclosure rights

content.question:
The main trade-off between capitalism and socialism is often described as "efficiency vs. equality" or "freedom vs. security."

Why might different people—or the same person at different life stages—prefer different balances?

What aspects of each system do you value most?

Consider the Martinez family story: Carlos built a $600,000 business in 30 years, while his Cuban relatives earn $30-50/month.

What specific features of the US economic system enabled Carlos's success?

What financial opportunities are available to you that wouldn't exist in a command economy?

questions.question: What's the key difference between how capitalism and socialism answer "who decides what to produce"?
Why are property rights essential for entrepreneurship and investment? Give two examples.
Why do countries with more economic freedom tend to have higher incomes?
What makes the U.S. a "mixed economy" rather than pure capitalism?

slides.notes:
## Answer Key

**Question 1:** B (Through voluntary exchanges between buyers and sellers—prices coordinate decisions). In capitalism, millions of individual decisions by consumers and producers determine what gets made. In socialism, central planners make these decisions.

**Question 2:** Property rights enable: (1) **Home equity building**—you can own, improve, and sell your home; mortgage possible because lender can foreclose if you default; (2) **Business ownership**—you keep profits, can sell business, use it as loan collateral; (3) **Investment**—buy stocks knowing companies can't arbitrarily seize your ownership shares. Without property rights, lenders can't foreclose on homes or seize business assets if borrowers default, so banks won't lend because they have no way to recover money. This eliminates mortgages and business loans for most people.

**Question 3:** Countries with more economic freedom have higher incomes because: (1) Property rights encourage investment and entrepreneurship; (2) Market prices coordinate resources efficiently; (3) Competition drives innovation and quality; (4) Profit incentives motivate productivity; (5) Economic freedom typically correlates with political freedom and rule of law. The data is clear: South Korea vs. North Korea (19x income gap), West vs. East Germany (3x gap), Chile vs. Venezuela show that systems matter more than culture, geography, or natural resources.

**Question 4:** B (Mixed economy—mostly private ownership but with government providing public goods, regulation, and social programs). The US has private businesses alongside public schools, Social Security, Medicare, FDA regulation, environmental laws, antitrust enforcement, etc. About 25% of GDP involves government spending.

**Additional Context:**
- **Trade-off:** Freedom vs. security. Capitalism offers more individual choice and wealth potential but less guaranteed security; socialism offers more security but restricts freedom and typically produces less prosperity. Different people prefer different balances based on their risk tolerance, confidence in abilities, and values.
- Personal opportunities in the US system include: starting businesses, choosing careers, negotiating salaries, investing in stocks/real estate, building wealth through property ownership, accessing credit markets.

takeaways.title: Systems Shape Opportunities
takeaways.description: Economic systems determine your career freedom, business opportunities, and wealth-building potential.

takeaways.title: Property Rights Enable Wealth
takeaways.description: Without secure property rights, mortgages, business loans, and wealth accumulation become impossible.

takeaways.title: Markets Coordinate Efficiently
takeaways.description: Prices convey information that central planners can't replicate—coordinating millions of decisions automatically.

takeaways.title: Mixed Economy = Opportunity + Safety Net
takeaways.description: The US combines market freedom with government programs. Success depends on your decisions within this framework.

main.title: Economic Systems Comparison Lab
main.description: You've learned how economic systems work—now analyze real countries and design your ideal system.

steps: Compare market capitalism, command socialism, and mixed economy characteristics
Analyze how a software developer's opportunities differ across systems
Evaluate trade-offs: freedom vs. security, efficiency vs. equality
Design your ideal mixed economy using sliders for ownership, taxes, and programs
Compare your design to the actual U.S. system—what would you change?

content.tagline: Building Financial Futures, One Lesson at a Time
